This fall, I begin my undergraduate studies in Neuroscience & Computer Science at the University of Texas at Austin, exploring the frontier of natural and artificial intelligence.

01 — About

A short introduction

Harvard Radcliffe
Institute · 2025
President · Writing & Publication NDEC · 19th EC

I'm a researcher and writer from Dhaka who believes the most interesting work happens where disciplines collide — where technology meets policy, and where data meets human stories.

My work spans peer-reviewed research on AI and sustainable supply chains, award-winning essays on economics and the environment, and on-the-ground climate and public-health advocacy across Bangladesh. I've co-founded a real estate firm, led one of the country's largest student clubs, and helped draft national climate policy as a UNDP fellow.

At UT Austin, I'll study Neuroscience and Computer Science — two fields I see as the next frontier for understanding intelligence, both natural and artificial.

03 — Research

Published & ongoing work

Co-authored studies at the intersection of artificial intelligence, economics, and sustainable development — built on field data and rigorous quantitative methods.

01
Big Data Analytics & AI for Sustainable Performance through Supply Chain Innovation
Published Springer Nature — Discover Sustainability SmartPLS · SEM
Co-authored study analyzing how AI-driven big data capabilities shape sustainable performance, drawing on interviews with 346 managers across Tejgaon textile industries. Led to a Distinguished Speaker invitation at NANO Intellects 2026. DOI ↗
View details
Method
Structural Equation Modeling in SmartPLS, with bootstrap resampling on a 346-manager survey across Tejgaon textile firms.
Sample
346 managers across small, mid-sized, and large textile manufacturers in Tejgaon, Dhaka.
Key Finding
AI-driven big data analytics improves sustainable performance — but only when paired with supply chain innovation as a mediator; technology alone is not enough.
My Role
Co-authored the manuscript, supported field data collection, and assisted with SmartPLS modeling and interpretation.
2024–25
02
Digital Pathways to Development: Women, Facebook Commerce & Socio-Economic Transformation
Published Emerald Publishing PLS-SEM · CB-SEM · Regression
Co-authored research based on interviews with 350 rural women, examining how social commerce drives economic transformation in emerging contexts. Led methodology and references. DOI ↗
View details
Method
Mixed quantitative approach: PLS-SEM and CB-SEM in tandem, with regression-based robustness checks.
Sample
350 rural women entrepreneurs operating informal Facebook-based commerce across multiple districts in Bangladesh.
Key Finding
Social commerce platforms materially improve household income and decision-making autonomy for rural women — with digital literacy and trust as the strongest enablers.
My Role
Led methodology design, structured the references and literature review, and contributed to the framing of the digital-pathways conceptual model.
2025–26
03
Driving Positive Word-of-Mouth: CSR, Customer Experience & Technology Innovation
Under Review AMOS · SEM
Co-authored SEM study of 372 respondents, identifying technological innovation as a key moderator that amplifies CSR's impact on customer advocacy — with a novel moderated-mediation model linking CSR and technology to loyalty.
View details
Method
Structural Equation Modeling in AMOS with moderated-mediation analysis; convergent and discriminant validity established prior to path testing.
Sample
372 customers of consumer-facing brands surveyed on CSR perceptions, customer experience, and word-of-mouth behaviors.
Key Finding
Technology innovation does not just contribute additively — it moderates the CSR-to-advocacy pathway, magnifying CSR's effect when innovation is strong.
My Role
Co-authored the manuscript, designed survey constructs, and ran the AMOS-based SEM modeling.
2024
04
Project Genesis: Scenario Analysis of Advanced Genetic Engineering in Agriculture
Under Review Monte Carlo · Python
Co-authored modeling study using Monte Carlo simulations in Python to project U.S. corn yields and revenues across four biotech adoption scenarios, with a risk-assessment framework translating outcomes into policy guidance.
View details
Method
Monte Carlo simulation in Python (NumPy + SciPy) over four biotech adoption scenarios, with sensitivity analysis on yield, price, and adoption-rate parameters.
Scope
U.S. corn production system. Projections of yields and revenues across baseline, partial-adoption, full-adoption, and accelerated genetic-engineering scenarios.
Key Finding
Accelerated adoption of advanced genetic engineering shows the highest expected yield gains but also the widest uncertainty band — making targeted, staged adoption more attractive than blanket policy.
My Role
Co-authored the paper and contributed to building the simulation framework and risk-assessment translation layer for policy implications.
2025
05
The Sovereignty Paradox: Why We Are Losing the $10.5 Trillion War on Cybercrime
Inspire Youth Journal — 23rd Edition Hall of Fame Writer
Authored a long-form analytical feature on the jurisdictional gaps that let cross-border cybercrime flourish, examining international conventions and the structural "sovereignty paradox" behind a projected $10.5 trillion in annual damage. Read in the 23rd Edition ↗
View details
Format
Long-form analytical feature for a youth-policy journal, structured around three case-driven sections and a closing prescriptive frame.
Core Argument
National sovereignty norms — designed for physical borders — are the structural reason cybercrime remains the world's most profitable shadow economy.
Evidence Base
Drew on Budapest Convention literature, UN cybersecurity reports, and projected damage estimates from Cybersecurity Ventures and the World Economic Forum.
Recognition
Named a Hall of Fame Writer by the Inspire Youth Journal editorial board.
2025
06
The $188 Billion Pivot: An Economic and Environmental Overhaul by EVs and Hybrids
Inspire Youth Journal — 25th Edition Recognized by John Locke Institute
Authored a feature on the global electric-vehicle transition — the investment and jobs it generates, its emissions impact, and the uneven pace of adoption between wealthy and developing nations. Read in the 25th Edition ↗
View details
Format
Feature-length policy essay weaving economic projections with environmental case data.
Core Argument
The $188 billion the EV-and-hybrid transition is projected to unlock is not just a green dividend — it's an industrial pivot that reshapes labor, supply chains, and energy geopolitics.
Evidence Base
IEA outlook reports, BloombergNEF EV market forecasts, and emissions-trajectory data from the IPCC AR6 working groups.
Recognition
Recognized by the John Locke Institute alongside contemporaneous award entries.
2026
